item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20245 Exploitation, codfish GitHub Action Compromise, and Edgecution Malware
codfish GitHub Action Compromise, Operation Endgame, and Exploited Ubiquiti CVE-2026-34908–34910
Bajaj ransomware, Cordyceps GitHub flaws, Mistic and SharkLoader campaigns
Cisco CVE-2026-20230 Exploitation, Huione Seizure, and Bashe’s Fake Hargreaves Lansdown Claim
Operation Endgame Takedown of Amadey & StealC Malware-as-a-Service; CISA Warns of Ubiquiti UniFi and Lantronix Exploits; Codfish GitHub Action Compromised
item.content:encoded:
codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action has been compromised — Must Read

The GitHub Action codfish/semantic-release-action was compromised via force-moved version tags, causing workflows using those tags to execute an obfuscated Bun-based payload that steals tokens, abuses GitHub APIs for C2/exfiltration, and attempts to spread through repositories, developer machines, SSH hosts, and package ecosystems. Users should assume any workflow run using affected tags after June 24, 2026 15:39 UTC may be compromised and rotate credentials/investigate immediately.

  • On June 24, 2026 at 15:39:06 UTC, an attacker force-pushed a malicious commit to codfish/semantic-release-action and redirected several version tags to point at that commit.
  • Workflows that ran codfish/semantic-release-action using affected tags after the tag changes executed a Bun-based, heavily obfuscated JavaScript payload inside the GitHub Actions runner.
  • The payload steals GitHub OIDC tokens, searches for Personal Access Tokens matching known GitHub token patterns, encrypts collected material with AES-128-GCM, and attempts to propagate a backdoor into other repositories accessible with stolen credentials.
  • The attacker changed the action from a Docker-based runner to a composite action that installs Bun with oven-sh/setup-bun and runs index.js, with both injected steps guarded by if: always().
  • The article states that StepSecurity added codfish/semantic-release-action to its Compromised Actions Policy and that Harden-Runner observed the malware using GitHub API traffic for command-and-control and exfiltration.

Mandiant reveals how Cisco SD-WAN zero-day attacks gained root access — Must Read

Mandiant revealed that attackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 after gaining device access, using a crafted CSV upload to create rogue root accounts and then erase traces. Cisco has issued fixes, and organizations should upgrade and check for unauthorized peering or other compromise indicators.

  • Mandiant revealed that hackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerability CVE-2026-20245 in zero-day attacks to create rogue root accounts on targeted devices.
  • CVE-2026-20245 is a high-severity command injection flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, Controller, and Validator that allows authenticated attackers with local access to execute arbitrary commands as root by uploading a crafted file.
  • Mandiant said the intrusion began with unauthorized SD-WAN peering connections on a service provider's infrastructure, followed by authentication to affected SD-WAN Manager devices using the vmanage-admin account.
  • The attackers exploited CVE-2026-20245 through a tenant-upload feature in the SD-WAN command-line interface by uploading a malicious CSV file named evil_tenant.csv.
  • Mandiant reported that the attackers used anti-forensic tactics, including restoring modified configuration files, deleting the malicious payload and temporary files, and erasing evidence of the rogue root account.

CISA Warns Critical Lantronix EDS5000 Flaw Is Being Actively Exploited — Must Read

CISA says a critical Lantronix EDS5000 command-injection flaw is being actively exploited and ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch by June 26, 2026. The agency also confirmed active exploitation of three critical Ubiquiti UniFi OS flaws that can be chained for root-level remote code execution.

  • CISA warned that CVE-2025-67038, a critical Lantronix EDS5000 Series code injection flaw with a CVSS score of 9.8, is being actively exploited.
  • CVE-2025-67038 allows attackers to inject arbitrary operating system commands into the username parameter, and the injected commands are executed with root privileges.
  • CISA urged Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to apply fixes for the Lantronix EDS5000 flaw by June 26, 2026.
  • CISA also confirmed active exploitation of three maximum-severity Ubiquiti UniFi OS flaws tracked as CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910.
  • Bishop Fox detailed a proof-of-concept that chains the three UniFi OS flaws to obtain a reverse shell with full root privileges in a single request, and Ubiquiti released patches late last month.

Cisco Unified CM Flaw Exploited After PoC Reveals File-Write Path to Root — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical Cisco Unified CM flaw that can let unauthenticated attackers write files and potentially escalate to root when WebDialer is enabled. Cisco has patched the issue in Unified CM/SME 14SU6 and 15SU5, and organizations should patch or disable WebDialer immediately.

  • Threat actors have begun exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical flaw affecting Cisco Unified Communications Manager and Unified Communications Manager Session Management Edition.
  • Cisco said the flaw is caused by improper input validation for specific HTTP requests and could allow an unauthenticated remote attacker to conduct server-side request forgery attacks through an affected device.
  • A successful exploit could allow an attacker to write files to the underlying operating system that could later be used to elevate to root.
  • Successful exploitation requires the Cisco WebDialer Web Service to be enabled, and Cisco said the service is disabled by default.
  • Cisco patched the vulnerability in Unified CM and Unified CM SME versions 14SU6 and 15SU5, and advised disabling WebDialer if immediate patching is not possible.

Malicious Edge extension abuses Native Messaging as bridge to malware — Must Read

A malicious Microsoft Edge extension called Edgecution is being used in ransomware-linked attacks to abuse Chrome Native Messaging, escape the browser sandbox, and launch a Python backdoor on infected Windows systems. The campaign starts with fake IT support messages on Microsoft Teams directing victims to a fraudulent Microsoft update page.

  • A malicious Microsoft Edge extension dubbed Edgecution has been used in a ransomware attack to escape the browser sandbox and deploy a Python-based backdoor.
  • The compromise begins with an attacker posing as IT support on Microsoft Teams and directing employees to a fraudulent page presented as a spam filter or Microsoft Outlook update site.
  • Zscaler researchers believe Edgecution is deployed by an initial access broker connected to the Payouts Kings ransomware operation.
  • Edgecution abuses Chrome Native Messaging to let the malicious Edge extension communicate with a local Python-based backdoor that can execute shell commands, run PowerShell, run arbitrary Python code, write files, enumerate processes, and gather system information.
  • Zscaler recommends that organizations strengthen monitoring of browser extensions and enforce strict controls over native messaging host configurations.

Stealthy Mistic backdoor linked to ransomware access broker KongTuke — Must Read

A new stealthy backdoor called Mistic has been linked to KongTuke/Woodgnat, an initial access broker that sells corporate network access to ransomware groups. The malware is built for long-term persistence, in-memory execution, credential theft, and expanding attacker capabilities while minimizing detection.

  • A new backdoor called Mistic has been observed in financially motivated attacks targeting organizations in the insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors.
  • Symantec believes Mistic is linked to KongTuke/Woodgnat, an initial access broker that sells compromised corporate network access to ransomware groups including Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base, and Black Basta.
  • In attacks investigated by Symantec, the infection chain used the legitimate MpExtMs.exe executable to side-load a malicious version.dll loader for Mistic, also named EndpointDlp.dll.
  • Mistic can upload, download, move, rename, and delete files, create folders, change its command-checking frequency, execute code in memory, and terminate itself while deleting files from the host.
  • Zscaler tracks Mistic as MTLBackdoor and reported that it was delivered as a payload in a multi-stage ClickFix infection chain in May.

Amadey and StealC Malware Network Disrupted, 27M Stolen Credentials Recovered — Must Read

International law enforcement and private-sector partners disrupted the Amadey and StealC malware-as-a-service networks, taking down hundreds of servers and domains while recovering 27 million stolen credentials. The operation targeted key infrastructure used to deliver infostealers, enable fraud, and support broader cybercrime operations.

  • A coordinated law enforcement operation with private sector partners including Bitdefender, Bitsight, ESET, and Microsoft disrupted criminal infrastructure powering Amadey and StealC.
  • The two-week action recovered 27 million stolen login credentials, dismantled 326 servers and 142 domains, and identified, flagged, and restricted more than $47 million in cryptocurrency assets of criminal origin.
  • Amadey and StealC are advertised under a malware-as-a-service model, with Amadey functioning as a loader and StealC stealing sensitive data such as credentials, session cookies, credit card data, browsing history, and extension data.
  • Microsoft said Amadey and StealC were linked to more than 140,000 infected computers globally in the first two weeks of May 2026 and that it identified over 18,000 victim computers and severed criminal control of those devices.
  • The operation took place between June 15 and 19, 2026, as part of Operation Endgame and involved judicial authorities and law enforcement from Belgium, Canada, Denmark, France, Germany, the Netherlands, the U.K., and the U.S.

Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks — Should Read

Researchers found a CI/CD workflow weakness dubbed Cordyceps that could let unauthenticated attackers hijack GitHub Actions workflows, steal credentials, and compromise more than 300 major open-source repositories. Affected projects included repositories tied to Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation, with several vendors confirming or patching issues after disclosure.

  • Novee Security identified a CI/CD workflow weakness called Cordyceps that can allow unauthenticated attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains.
  • A scan of about 30,000 high-impact repositories found more than 300 to be fully exploitable, enabling attacker-controlled code execution, credential theft, and supply chain compromise.
  • The issue stems from weak CI/CD configurations that grant pull requests more permissions than they should have, allowing untrusted pull requests to trigger privileged workflows.
  • Novee reported examples affecting repositories associated with Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation.
  • After responsible disclosure, Microsoft and Google confirmed impact, while Cloudflare, Python, and Apache applied hardening or patches.

StrikeShark: investigating a new campaign delivering Cobalt Strike through SharkLoader — Should Read

Kaspersky uncovered StrikeShark, a previously undocumented campaign using the new SharkLoader malware to deploy Cobalt Strike against government, diplomatic, software development, and other organizations across multiple countries. The attackers gain access through vulnerable internet-facing applications and malicious droppers, then use sophisticated in-memory loading, API hooking, and persistence techniques to evade detection.

  • Kaspersky uncovered a previously undocumented malware family named SharkLoader while investigating activity affecting a diplomatic organization in Indonesia.
  • SharkLoader is used to deploy Cobalt Strike Beacon and has been delivered through exploited internet-facing applications including Microsoft Exchange, Microsoft SharePoint, Openfire Server, and GeoServer, as well as through custom droppers disguised as legitimate software.
  • The StrikeShark campaign affected government-related entities, software development companies, and other organizations in countries including Indonesia, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Lebanon, Syria, Colombia, North Macedonia, Nepal, and Serbia.
  • SharkLoader uses DLL sideloading, encrypted in-memory components, API hooks, and memory protection changes around Cobalt Strike Beacon sleep periods to support execution and evasion.
  • The researchers found no code or infrastructure overlap linking SharkLoader to a known threat actor, but assessed the campaign as likely Chinese-speaking with low confidence based on the open-source tools used after compromise.

Trust No One: Automating macOS Privilege Escalation at Scale — Should Read

The article describes a macOS privilege escalation technique that lets a standard user impersonate trusted app components through CDHash cache and NIB injection abuse, enabling privileged XPC calls that can disable EDR and MDM agents. It argues developers must validate XPC client identity on each connection rather than relying on cached code-signing trust.

  • The article describes a macOS privilege escalation technique that can allow standard user accounts to silently disable enterprise security products, including EDR and MDM solutions, without administrator credentials or kernel exploits.
  • The technique chains CDHash kernel cache exploitation with NIB payload injection to impersonate trusted application components and invoke privileged XPC methods without authentication.
  • The research was validated against CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor, where full sensor unloading was achieved from a standard user account, and Kandji MDM Agent, where permanent agent deactivation was achieved through a two-phase XPC chain.
  • The author built XPC Hunter, an open-source automated framework intended to discover exploitable XPC privilege escalation surfaces across installed macOS applications at scale.
  • The article recommends that developers validate client identity inside XPC listener logic using xpc_connection_set_peer_code_signing_requirement() or by re-validating signatures with SecCodeCheckValidity() and audit-token-based checks.

DoJ Seizes Huione Cloud Account Tied to Cyber Scam Money Laundering — Should Read

The U.S. Justice Department seized a cloud account used by HuiOne Group subsidiaries to support money laundering infrastructure tied to crypto fraud, scam compounds, and an illicit Telegram marketplace. Authorities also expanded pressure on related Southeast Asian scam networks, including sanctions against Prince Group-linked individuals and entities.

  • The U.S. Department of Justice announced the seizure of a cloud computing account used by subsidiaries of Cambodia-based HuiOne Group to host backend infrastructure for alleged money laundering services.
  • The seized account supported HuiOne Guarantee, also known as Haowang Guarantee, an illicit Telegram-based marketplace that handled billions of dollars in transactions between 2021 and 2025.
  • The marketplace offered personal and financial data, money laundering services, fraudulent investment and phishing website services, human trafficking procurement, and software for face swapping, voice cloning, and deepfake impersonation during video calls.
  • FinCEN assessed H-Pay Service PLC as a primary money laundering concern to guard against HuiOne Group’s attempts to circumvent being cut off from the U.S. financial system.
  • The U.S. Treasury imposed sanctions against nine individuals and 26 entities linked to Prince Group, which had been classified as a Transnational Criminal Organization for its role in scam compounds, fraud, and money laundering.

German rail services resume after wireless communications outage — Should Read

Deutsche Bahn restored rail services after a nationwide outage in its GSM-R railway radio system halted trains across Germany for about two hours. The company said the disruption was caused by a technical failure during scheduled component replacement, not a reported cyberattack.

  • Deutsche Bahn restored train services early Wednesday after a technical failure in its railway communications network halted rail traffic across Germany for roughly two hours overnight.
  • The outage affected long-distance and regional trains as well as S-Bahn commuter services, and Deutsche Bahn warned passengers to expect lingering delays and cancellations after service resumed.
  • Deutsche Bahn said the incident stemmed from a malfunction in its GSM-R digital railway radio system, which is used by train drivers, dispatchers, and signaling systems to coordinate rail operations safely.
  • Philipp Nagl said the disruption occurred during a scheduled replacement of a technical component, and Deutsche Bahn said it was still analyzing the exact cause.
  • The company has not indicated that the incident was the result of a cyberattack and has described it as a technical failure.

Indian auto giant Bajaj Auto hit by ransomware incident — Should Read

Bajaj Auto disclosed a ransomware incident affecting its operations and technology subsidiary, saying containment and mitigation efforts are underway and so far successful. The company has not identified the attackers or said whether data was stolen or a ransom was demanded.

  • Bajaj Auto disclosed in a regulatory filing that it was hit by a ransomware attack affecting its operations and Bajaj Auto Technology Limited.
  • The company said it became aware of the incident on Tuesday morning and took precautionary measures to contain its impact.
  • Bajaj Auto said its technical team and cybersecurity experts responded immediately and that mitigation efforts had so far been successful.
  • Bajaj Auto did not disclose the threat actor’s identity, whether any data was stolen, or whether a ransom demand was issued.
  • The disclosure follows other cyber incidents involving major Indian manufacturers, including a cybersecurity incident confirmed earlier in the week by Tata Electronics.

A Free Gmail Account Impersonating an Internal Employee Asked Payroll to Change a Bank Account — May Read

A payroll redirect BEC used a free Gmail account with a spoofed display name to impersonate an internal employee and request a direct-deposit bank change, bypassing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC because there were no links, attachments, or malicious code. The key defense is mandatory out-of-band verification for any banking-change request.

  • An attacker used a free Gmail account, churchoffice11011[at]gmail[.]com, with a display name matching a real internal employee at a financial institution to contact the payroll recipient.
  • The email asked for a direct-deposit bank account update and contained no links, attachments, malicious code, urgency marker, deadline, or threat.
  • SPF, DKIM, and DMARC all passed for Gmail infrastructure because the message was legitimately sent through Gmail and the deception was limited to the display name.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 75% confidence and labeled it as BEC and Vendor Email Compromise based on the mismatch between the claimed employee identity and the unrelated consumer Gmail address.
  • The article recommends out-of-band verification for all direct-deposit, ACH, or wire changes by calling the employee at a known, pre-existing number before processing the request.

UK Cybercrime Journal: Hargreaves Landsdown Extortion Attempt by Bashe — May Read

Hargreaves Lansdown was named by Bashe/APT73 in an alleged data-theft extortion attempt, but analysis suggests the claimed customer database was fabricated using previously leaked public breach data. The key takeaway is that threat actors are increasingly exploiting real IT outages and customer anxiety to create convincing fake breach narratives for extortion.

  • Hargreaves Lansdown experienced customer-facing technical issues in September 2025 and March 2026, including incorrect displayed balances and access problems with its website and app.
  • On 27 April 2026, Bashe listed Hargreaves Lansdown on its Tor data leak site and claimed to have stolen a customer database containing 658,259 unique users.
  • On 8 May 2026, alleged Hargreaves Lansdown data appeared on DarkForums, with a posted sample containing names, addresses, emails, phone numbers, and dates of birth but no customer account numbers or transaction details.
  • The article states that sample email addresses appeared in the Verifications.io breach and the People Data Labs customer breach, which it says likely indicates the Bashe and DarkForums data was fake.
  • The article assesses with high confidence that the alleged Hargreaves Lansdown data breach was fabricated and that Bashe used the company’s recent IT outages to support a false extortion narrative.

Disposable Tooling: Building LLM-Generated Mythic Agents from Prompt to Deployment — May Read

SpecterOps demonstrates that with the right testing harness and feedback loops, LLMs can generate and validate disposable Mythic C2 agents from prompt to deployment in a few hours. The key takeaway is that rapidly generated, single-use offensive tooling is now practical, making static implant signatures increasingly unreliable for defenders.

  • The author explored generating “disposable tooling” by using LLMs to build Mythic agents from an initial prompt through deployment.
  • The first attempt with Claude Code and Opus 4.6 produced code that compiled and deployed as a Docker image, but the generated Mythic agent failed because of missing packages, incorrect paths, hallucinated Mythic RPC methods, and misunderstandings of Mythic key exchange.
  • Adding Mythic documentation as an Anthropic Skill improved results and helped produce a working proof-of-concept agent, but still required some manual intervention and steering.
  • The author created an Oracle harness with tiered testing, including a mock Mythic server, a live Mythic server and Windows test host, LabKit, Mythicd, and a QA sub-agent to improve validation and feedback.
  • Using the improved harness, the author generated multiple basic Stage-0 Mythic agents in different languages with Claude and GPT Cyber models, typically in about 1.5 to just over 2 hours.

DraftKings hacker 'Snoopy' sentenced to 18 months in prison — May Read

Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old hacker known as “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for helping compromise tens of thousands of DraftKings accounts in a 2022 credential-stuffing scheme. The attackers stole about $600,000, and Austad was also ordered to pay forfeiture and restitution.

  • Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old from Minnesota who used the alias “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for his role in hacking DraftKings accounts in the November 2022 cyberattack.
  • Austad pleaded guilty in December 2025 to conspiracy to commit computer intrusion and admitted that he and co-conspirators compromised 60,000 DraftKings user accounts.
  • During the attack, the hackers added payment methods under their control to 1,600 accounts and stole $600,000.
  • U.S. authorities said Austad operated his own shop named after Snoopy where he sold access to stolen accounts, and his cryptocurrency accounts received approximately $465,000 in assets.
  • In addition to prison, Austad received three years of supervised release and was ordered to pay $463,684 in forfeiture and $1,327,061 in restitution.

Why Is an AI Image Company Building a Body Scanner? — May Read

Midjourney plans to roll out a consumer full-body ultrasound scanner in spas without FDA clearance yet, raising significant cybersecurity, data volume, and regulatory concerns. Health-ISAC warns that users may not receive HIPAA protections if they are treated as customers rather than patients.

  • Midjourney has announced its first hardware product, a full-body ultrasound scanner that it plans to deploy in consumer spas.
  • The article states that Midjourney has no FDA clearance yet for the planned consumer rollout.
  • Phil Englert of Health-ISAC said a single full-body ultrasound study can produce roughly 800 terabytes of data, about three times what a midsize hospital generates in a day.
  • Englert said the scanner’s thousands of sound-emitting and receiving elements add to the attack surface, and a threat model would need to consider whether transducers could be manipulated during a scan.
  • Englert said a spa that charges customers directly without involving insurance may fall outside HIPAA rules, so users should understand whether they are customers or patients and what protections apply.

Unlocking the Cloudflare app ecosystem with OAuth for all — May Read

Cloudflare has opened self-managed OAuth to all customers, enabling developers to build delegated-access integrations with clearer consent and revocation instead of relying on API tokens. The launch followed major OAuth engine upgrades and migrations to improve security, reliability, and performance.

  • Cloudflare announced self-managed OAuth so customers can create and manage their own OAuth clients for delegated access to the Cloudflare API.
  • Before this release, third-party OAuth was limited to a small number of manually onboarded integrations, so many developers building integrations had to rely on API tokens.
  • Cloudflare updated its OAuth consent experience, added dashboard revocation, and made app ownership more visible to help users understand and control application access.
  • Cloudflare upgraded its underlying Hydra OAuth engine using a staged approach, including custom migrations and a blue-green migration strategy to reduce user impact and preserve revocations.
  • After the Hydra upgrade, Cloudflare reported improved performance metrics, including lower API P95 latency, memory use, heap allocation, goroutines, and CPU usage.

How much cyber risk does AI create for organizations? 457 million security issues. Here’s what you can do about it. — Optional

Tenable says AI adoption, especially unapproved “shadow AI,” is creating massive security exposure beyond traditional CVEs, with 457 million AI-related issues detected across 7,000+ organizations in 30 days. The key takeaway is that organizations need continuous AI-driven exposure management to find misconfigurations, exposed credentials, unmanaged dependencies, and attack paths before attackers exploit them.

  • Tenable detected 457 million AI-related security issues across more than 7,000 organizations during a 30-day period, averaging 62,000 exposures per organization.
  • The article says AI-related security issues are primarily tied to misconfigurations and unmanaged dependencies rather than standard CVEs.
  • Tenable telemetry showed that some organizations remained exposed to older vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-21762 in Fortinet FortiOS, CVE-2021-44228 Log4Shell, and CVE-2017-0144 WannaCry.
  • The article states that about 37% of Tenable findings are not CVEs but account for 63% of breach entry points.
  • The article argues that security teams should use AI-driven exposure management to continuously assess vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, excessive permissions, exposed secrets, and attack paths across IT, OT, AI, IoT, and cloud assets.

How to evaluate Cloud Detection & Response (CDR) platforms and vendors — Optional

The article provides a checklist for evaluating Cloud Detection and Response platforms, emphasizing that buyers should prioritize broad visibility across cloud, SaaS, identity, and AI systems, plus fast, context-aware response. The key test is whether a platform can measurably reduce detection time, response time, and blast radius in real cloud attacks.

  • The article says Cloud Detection and Response platforms should be evaluated based on real operational needs rather than simple feature lists.
  • A CDR platform should provide visibility across cloud control planes, SaaS, identity providers, workloads, storage, databases, APIs, networking logs, and configuration changes.
  • The article emphasizes that AI-driven triage, cross-domain correlation, automatic attack path reconstruction, and machine-speed detection should reduce noise and give analysts clarity.
  • Identity telemetry is described as central to cloud detection because attackers with valid credentials can bypass traditional network-based assumptions.
  • The article says organizations should choose a CDR platform by whether it reduces time-to-detect, time-to-respond, and blast radius across critical cloud, SaaS, and identity paths.

The most aggressive AI adoption in healthcare is happening off the books — Optional

Healthcare workers are rapidly adopting unapproved “shadow AI” tools to save time, but this creates serious risks around data breaches, privacy misuse, and lack of organizational oversight. The key challenge is building governance that manages this usage rather than trying to stop it outright.

  • The article discusses unapproved “off the books” AI tools, also called “shadow AI,” becoming widespread in healthcare organizations.
  • The article says these unapproved AI tools are difficult to track within healthcare organizations.
  • The article states that shadow AI may save individuals valuable time but can expose organizations to data breaches and misuse of private data.
  • The article is written by Errol Weiss, chief security officer of Health-ISAC.
  • The article highlights governance challenges, including why governance keeps losing the race and why shadow AI is harder to contain than shadow IT.

Securing the service desk: Why social engineering attacks keep succeeding — Optional

Service desks remain a high-value target for social engineering because attackers can trick support staff into resetting credentials or MFA, gaining access faster than by breaking technical defenses. Organizations should harden identity verification, limit help desk privileges, monitor reset activity, and train staff against phone and chat-based impersonation.

  • Service desk social engineering remains one of the most effective ways for attackers to gain access to corporate systems.
  • In the Marks & Spencer case, Chairman Archie Norman confirmed that attackers impersonated an employee and convinced a third-party service desk agent to reset credentials, providing access to internal systems.
  • Attackers target service desks because staff can reset passwords, provision accounts, or disable multi-factor authentication, giving attackers a direct path to legitimate access.
  • A typical service desk attack can involve reconnaissance, impersonation, credential reset or MFA bypass, access and lateral movement, and eventual ransomware deployment or data theft.
  • The article recommends strict identity verification, stronger MFA reset controls, service desk training, monitoring of reset activity, limited help desk privileges, and regular reviews of outsourced service desk arrangements.

Insufficient Egress Filtering: How Weak Outbound Controls Enable Attacks — Optional

Weak egress filtering lets compromised systems freely communicate with attacker infrastructure and exfiltrate data. Organizations should tightly control and monitor outbound traffic to reduce command-and-control, lateral movement, and data theft risks.

  • The article is about insufficient egress filtering.
  • The article describes weak outbound controls as a factor that can enable attacks.
  • The article focuses on controls for traffic leaving a network.

Dawn of the Apex Agentic Adversary — Optional

The article warns that agentic AI will drastically compress the time from vulnerability discovery to exploitation, making traditional catalog- and signature-based defense less effective. It argues organizations must proactively map assets, validate segmentation, and close IT/OT blind spots before automated adversaries exploit them.

  • The article argues that frontier agentic AI models emerging in early 2026 can actively test code and compress the time between vulnerability discovery and weaponization.
  • The article says organizations have given AI tools deep access to infrastructure, including write access to code repositories and connections to internal APIs through third-party AI wrappers.
  • The article states that AI-driven breaches may become fast, targeted, and constantly changing enough that public tracking systems such as CISA’s KEV Catalog and EPSS may not capture them effectively.
  • The article warns that converged IT and OT environments can allow AI attackers to move from corporate systems into industrial systems through bridging devices and insecure-by-design protocols such as Modbus, BACnet, and S7comm.
  • The article presents runZero’s asset discovery, segmentation validation, attack path mapping, and risk prioritization capabilities as ways to reduce network blind spots and identify critical exposure paths.

item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20230 Exploited, FortiBleed Harvests Credentials, macOS ClickFix Pushes AMOS
Federal PQC deadlines, Scattered Spider plea, and AI agent supply-chain risks
